findings.summary: getUserWithTimeout accepts a _timeoutMs parameter (default 15000) that is completely ignored. The function was refactored from an async timeout-based approach to a synchronous localStorage read, but the parameter and Promise return type were kept for backward compatibility.
evidence: Line 106: export function getUserWithTimeout(_timeoutMs = 15000): Promise<{ data: { user: { id: string } | null }; error: null }> - underscore-prefixed param that is never used
Lines 107-121: function body is entirely synchronous (in-memory cache or localStorage read), yet returns a Promise wrapping the result
findings.suggestion: Remove the _timeoutMs parameter entirely since no caller passes it. Consider also making the function synchronous (returning the result directly) since it does no async work -- the Promise wrapper is unnecessary overhead. Callers already destructure the result immediately.
findings.reasoning: A parameter with a meaningful default value (15000) on a public export signals that callers should consider providing it, but it has no effect. This is misleading API surface. The async-looking return type for a synchronous function also adds conceptual overhead at every call site.

findings.summary: uploadImageWithThumbnail has a union-typed fourth parameter (callback | options object) and an unused third parameter (_userId), creating an inconsistent API surface.
evidence: Line 128-132: signature has _userId: string (unused, kept for 'backwards compat'), then onProgressOrOptions?: ((progress: number) => void) | UploadWithThumbnailOptions
Lines 134-140: runtime dispatch between function and object forms
findings.suggestion: Remove the unused _userId parameter and accept only the UploadWithThumbnailOptions object form. Update callers (likely few, given this is a utility) to pass { onProgress: fn } instead of a bare callback. This is a clean break that removes the dual-API surface.
findings.reasoning: Overloaded parameter types (function | object) force every reader to understand two calling conventions. The unused _userId parameter is dead weight that callers must still pass (even as empty string), which is error-prone.

findings.summary: generationTaskBridge.ts defines GenerationTaskMapping and TaskGenerationMapping interfaces that are never used by any consumer -- they exist only inside the file and have no external references.
evidence: Lines 25-29: GenerationTaskMapping interface defined but not exported or used
Lines 31-35: TaskGenerationMapping interface defined but not exported or used
Grep for these names shows they only appear in this file and its test file
findings.suggestion: Remove both unused interfaces. They appear to be aspirational types from the consolidation comment block that were never wired in.
findings.reasoning: Dead types in a 'centralized' bridge file create noise and suggest the API is wider than it actually is.

findings.summary: timelineWriteQueue.ts defines its own private isAbortError function, duplicating the shared isAbortError in errorUtils.ts. The module cannot import from errorUtils (or deliberately chose not to), but this creates divergent abort-detection logic.
evidence: timelineWriteQueue.ts lines 36-45: private isAbortError function with its own DOMException + message check logic
errorUtils.ts exports isAbortError which is imported by taskCreation.ts and other files
findings.suggestion: Import isAbortError from errorUtils.ts instead of maintaining a private copy. If the timeline module has a reason to diverge (e.g., different heuristics), add a comment explaining why.
findings.reasoning: Two independent implementations of the same concept will drift over time. If one is updated to handle a new edge case, the other will be missed.

findings.summary: readAccessTokenFromStorage (synchronous localStorage auth token read) is duplicated between taskCreation.ts and toolSettingsService.ts (as readUserIdFromStorage). Both parse the same sb-{ref}-auth-token key with slightly different extraction paths.
evidence: taskCreation.ts lines 17-28: readAccessTokenFromStorage extracts access_token from localStorage
toolSettingsService.ts lines 60-71: readUserIdFromStorage extracts user.id from the same localStorage key
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared readSupabaseSession() helper that parses the localStorage key once and returns the full session shape ({ access_token, user: { id } }). Each call site can then pick the field it needs without duplicating the parsing logic.
findings.reasoning: Both functions parse the same localStorage key with the same error handling pattern. When the storage key format changes, both must be updated in sync. A single parse point eliminates this coordination cost.

findings.summary: generateThumbnail in complete_task/storage.ts has three underscore-prefixed unused parameters (_sourceBytes, _userId, _taskId) that bloat its call signature.
evidence: Lines 143-149: function generateThumbnail(supabase, _sourceBytes: Uint8Array, _userId: string, _taskId: string, mainImageUrl: string, objectPath?: string)
The function only uses supabase, mainImageUrl, and objectPath
findings.suggestion: Remove _sourceBytes, _userId, and _taskId from the signature. They appear to be remnants of a previous implementation that uploaded a resized image manually. Update the single call site (handleThumbnail line 130) accordingly.
findings.reasoning: Three unused parameters on a private function is confusing -- it suggests the function needs data it actually ignores. This is especially misleading for _sourceBytes which implies byte processing.

findings.summary: useGenerationInvalidation.ts exports both a hook (useInvalidateGenerations) and a standalone function (invalidateGenerationsSync) that do the same thing but handle delay differently: the hook uses refs for timeout tracking, while the sync function uses raw setTimeout without cancellation. The invalidateVariantChange function is async (uses await) while the others are sync-returning-void.
evidence: Lines 58-81: useInvalidateGenerations hook with timeout ref tracking
Lines 84-97: invalidateGenerationsSync with bare setTimeout (no cleanup)
Lines 105-141: invalidateVariantChange is async with await, different return type (Promise<void>)
The hook variant clears previous timeouts for the same shotId; the sync variant does not
findings.suggestion: Consider unifying the delay mechanism. invalidateGenerationsSync should either not support delay (callers can wrap in setTimeout themselves) or share the debounce logic. The behavioral difference (hook cancels previous delays per shotId, sync does not) is a subtle bug vector.
findings.reasoning: Two functions with the same name pattern (invalidate generations) but different delay semantics at the same boundary is a maintenance trap. A caller switching from hook to sync form may not realize they lose debounce behavior.

findings.summary: useLineageChain.ts contains two functions (fetchLineageChain and getLineageDepth) that perform nearly identical traversal of the source_variant_id chain with separate visited-set and while-loop logic, duplicating the core iteration pattern.
evidence: Lines 36-74: fetchLineageChain walks source_variant_id chain, collecting full variant data
Lines 105-134: getLineageDepth walks the same chain collecting only depth count
Both use identical visited set, while loop, and params.source_variant_id extraction
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared walkLineageChain(variantId, selectFields) generator or callback-based traversal that both can use. getLineageDepth could call fetchLineageChain and return chain.length - 1, or a shared iterator could yield variant data while each consumer decides what to collect.
findings.reasoning: Duplicated traversal logic means fixing a bug in the chain-walking pattern (e.g., handling a new params shape) requires updating two places. The iteration pattern is identical -- only the data collected differs.

findings.summary: generateVideoService.ts is well-decomposed into helper functions, but the main generateVideo function and buildTravelRequestBody have very wide interfaces (GenerateVideoParams has 14 fields, buildTravelRequestBody takes 22 fields). The boundary between the service and its callers is broad.
evidence: Lines 126-143: GenerateVideoParams interface with 14 fields
Lines 437-459: buildTravelRequestBody parameter interface with 22 fields
Line 645: createTravelBetweenImagesTask called with requestBody as unknown as TravelBetweenImagesTaskParams -- type cast at the handoff
findings.suggestion: The type cast on line 645 (as unknown as TravelBetweenImagesTaskParams) indicates the request body shape doesn't actually match the declared task params type. Consider having buildTravelRequestBody return TravelBetweenImagesTaskParams directly (or a properly typed subset) to eliminate the unsafe cast. The wide param interfaces are acceptable for a complex domain, but the type escape at the final handoff undermines the work done to type everything above it.
findings.reasoning: A double type cast (as unknown as X) at the most critical boundary -- the actual task creation call -- defeats the purpose of the typed interfaces above it. If the shapes genuinely don't match, it suggests the interface contract is wrong somewhere.

findings.summary: cacheUtils.ts hardcodes SHOTS_CACHE_VARIANTS = [undefined, 0, 2, 5] to enumerate all possible maxImagesPerShot values used across the app. This creates a hidden coupling where adding a new variant elsewhere silently breaks cache consistency.
evidence: Lines 8-9: const SHOTS_CACHE_VARIANTS = [undefined, 0, 2, 5] as const; with comment explaining the magic numbers
Lines 11-17: getShotsCacheKeys builds keys for all variants
findings.suggestion: Consider using queryClient.getQueriesData with a predicate matching the shots.all prefix, rather than enumerating known variants. This would automatically cover any new variant without code changes. Alternatively, export SHOTS_CACHE_VARIANTS from the same file that defines the query keys, co-locating the constraint with its source of truth.
findings.reasoning: Magic number arrays that must be kept in sync with scattered call sites are a classic maintenance hazard. The comment already documents this fragility but doesn't solve it.

findings.summary: rateLimitHeaders in rateLimit.ts is a private function only used by rateLimitResponse. However, consumers who need to add rate limit headers to successful responses (200s) cannot access it -- they'd have to reconstruct the headers manually.
evidence: Lines 121-133: rateLimitHeaders is not exported, only used internally by rateLimitResponse
Lines 138-154: rateLimitResponse is the only way to get headers, but it always returns 429
findings.suggestion: Export rateLimitHeaders so consumers can attach rate limit info to successful responses as well. This is standard practice for rate-limited APIs (always return X-RateLimit-* headers, not just on 429).
findings.reasoning: Consumers who want to include rate limit headers on 200 responses (standard HTTP practice) cannot reuse the header-building logic, leading to either missing headers on success responses or duplicated header construction.
